text: The surgeon places robotic trocars and begins reducing the incarcerated umbilical hernia, noting adhesions of the omentum to the hernia sac. The peritoneal flap development starts in the falciform fat pad, moving approximately five centimeters away from the umbilicus to ensure broad mesh coverage. The dissection proceeds smoothly in the preperitoneal plane due to the favorable anatomy and presence of preperitoneal fat.

The surgeon uses a hand-over-hand paddling maneuver to develop the flap, carefully dissecting the fat away to identify the hernia sac. The fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand is used with strong grip to apply traction and reduce the hernia sac. A small hole in the peritoneum is visible, which will be addressed later.

The surgeon performs delicate dissection to reduce the hernia sac, carefully avoiding bleeding while identifying the correct plane to reduce the incarcerated pre-peritoneal fat. The procedure transitions to contralateral preperitoneal dissection, creating a broad pocket for mesh placement. The pre-cut mesh is prepared for a two-centimeter hernia.

The surgeon uses a V-Lock absorbable suture to close the umbilical defect in a transverse orientation. The bedside assistant uses a DeBakey instrument to push the umbilical stock into view for intermittent suturing. The surgeon takes bites through the umbilical stock and fascia, adjusting insufflation pressure between twelve and fifteen for optimal visualization.

The surgeon identifies a small defect superior to the umbilical hernia, opting not to close it due to its size and potential for tension. The V-lock suture is run backwards to lock the fascia closure, and Bard Soft Mesh is placed with the rough side facing the abdominal wall for adherence.

The surgeon centers the mesh within the prepared pocket, directing it cranially due to the patient's diastasis. Using a 3-0 polysorb suture, the mesh is fixed with interrupted stitches, typically three to four, ensuring secure placement. Additional sutures may be used to close any peritoneal holes. The mesh is cut to size, and instrument exchanges are minimized by pre-placing necessary items, including a 20 cm polysorb and two 6-inch V-locks.

The surgeon begins closure of the peritoneal flap using a baseball stitch technique, starting from one apex and sewing left to right. The initial bites are challenging due to limited assistance from the left hand, but the process becomes easier once central in the flap. The flap closure is completed, and the surgeon addresses a small fenestration using a V-lock suture to close the hole.
